keywords: primary color, primary palette, primary
answer: Three primary colors: Black (#000000) is the foundation, HCFM Blue (#0047BB / Pantone 2728C) is the identifier, Yellow Gold (#FFB500 / Pantone 7549C) is the energy.

keywords: secondary color, accent, accent palette
answer: Three secondary colors: Marian Blue (#00A9E0 / 2995C) pairs with Playlist Script. Muted Gold (#89764B / 871C) is for print and donor materials. White is for text on dark backgrounds.

keywords: liturgical, lent, advent, pentecost, ordinary time, extended
answer: Extended palette: Purple (#5F259F) for Lent, Advent, and penitential themes. Red (#CF3339) for Pentecost, martyrs, Sacred Heart. Green (#3A913F) for Ordinary Time, growth, creation care.

keywords: hcfm blue, pantone 2728, brand blue, identifier blue
answer: HCFM Blue. Hex #0047BB · RGB 0/71/187 · Pantone 2728C. The brand identifier — the logo, the mark, formal letterhead, official documents, website headers. Use sparingly outside those moments so it stays meaningful.

keywords: yellow gold, pantone 7549, gold hex, ffb500
answer: Yellow Gold. Hex #FFB500 · RGB 255/181/0 · Pantone 7549C. The energy color. Headlines on dark backgrounds, call-to-action buttons, accent borders, Playlist Script, icons that need to pop. Never for body text on white.

keywords: marian blue, pantone 2995, sky blue, light blue
answer: Marian Blue. Hex #00A9E0 · RGB 0/169/224 · Pantone 2995C. Pairs with Playlist Script. Closely associated with Mary — gentleness, presence, guidance. The brand book sometimes calls this "Light Blue" — same color.

keywords: muted gold, pantone 871, old gold, 89764b
answer: Muted Gold. Hex #89764B · RGB 137/118/75 · Pantone 871C. The reverent gold. Print, formal, donor materials, certificates. Symbolizes sacredness in traditional, devotional contexts.

keywords: black, foundation color, 000000
answer: Black (#000000). Process Black. Your workhorse. The primary background that lets every other brand color shine. Use it for social graphics, video thumbnails, and as a 40 to 70 percent overlay on photos.

keywords: white, ffffff, breathing space
answer: White (#FFFFFF). Visual rest and balance. The space between things. Use white as text on dark, and as negative space to let content breathe.

keywords: yellow gold vs muted gold, when use muted gold, two golds, gold difference
answer: Simple rule: If it is on a screen, use Yellow Gold. If it is for donors, on a certificate, or in formal print, use Muted Gold. Yellow Gold is digital and energetic. Muted Gold is traditional and reverent. The two golds are not interchangeable.

keywords: yellow gold body text, gold on white, accessibility, contrast
answer: Never use Yellow Gold for body text on white. Yellow Gold on white is 1.9:1 contrast — fails WCAG AA. Use Yellow Gold for headlines and accents only on dark backgrounds.

keywords: contrast, wcag, readable, accessibility ratio
answer: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text. HCFM Blue on White: 8.6:1 (AAA). White on HCFM Blue: 8.6:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on Black: 11.7:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on White: 1.9:1 (FAILS).

keywords: light blue, why marian
answer: "Light Blue" in the 2026 brand book and "Marian Blue" in the Visual Identity deck refer to the same color (#00A9E0). Going forward, we standardize on "Marian Blue" because it carries the Marian meaning explicitly.

keywords: extended palette, do not use, old colors
answer: The old SharePoint extended palette (red, orange, dark green, magenta, light pink, sage, etc. — 14 colors total) is deprecated. The 2026 system is six colors only: 4 primary, 2 accent, plus three liturgical (Purple, Red, Green) for seasonal use.

keywords: mark vs logo, mark or logo, difference mark logo, symbol vs logo
answer: The Mark is the symbol alone. Use when space is small or the audience already knows HCFM (favicons, profile pictures, watermarks). The Logo is the symbol with "Holy Cross Family Ministries." Use when introducing HCFM, or for official documents.

keywords: logotype 1, horizontal, wide layout
answer: Logotype 1 — Horizontal. Mark left, wordmark right on a single horizontal line. Best for wide layouts: web headers, document headers, email signatures.

keywords: logotype 2, stacked
answer: Logotype 2 — Stacked. Mark on top, wordmark on two lines below. The default formal version. Use on letterhead, certificates, official documents.

keywords: logotype 3, compact stacked
answer: Logotype 3 — Compact stacked. Mark on top with a smaller wordmark. For tight spaces where the stacked version is too tall but you still need full identification.

keywords: logotype 4, single line, narrow
answer: Logotype 4 — Single line. Mark with the full wordmark on a single line. Use in narrow horizontal contexts: footers, banners, sign-off blocks.

keywords: logo download, where logo, download logo, get logo
answer: Logo files are gated on the Downloads tab. Public packs include PNG and JPG only. Editable AI source files are restricted to brand owners (Victoria and Emmanuel). Email vhassan@hcfm.org or eepau@hcfm.org for access.

keywords: ai file, editable, illustrator, source file, eps
answer: AI / EPS / editable source files are restricted to the brand owners (Victoria and Emmanuel). This is intentional: a single canonical source keeps every ministry-center logo identical. If you need a custom edit, request it through the Help tab.

keywords: minimum size, logo size, how small
answer: Mark alone: 5/16 inch (7.94 mm) print, 150 px web. Wordmark "FAMILY MINISTRIES" cap height: 1/16 inch (1.59 mm). Below these sizes, legibility breaks.

keywords: clear space, free space, margin around logo
answer: Clear space around the mark equals the height of one prayer-person bead in the mark itself. Keep that minimum margin on all four sides — never let other elements crowd inside it.

keywords: logo do not, misuse, never do, logo rule
answer: Never: stretch or distort the logo · use colors outside the approved palette · substitute fonts on the wordmark · add drop shadows, glows, or 3D · add strokes or outlines · crop in a way that obscures the form · rotate the mark · place on busy backgrounds without an overlay.

keywords: file format, svg, png, jpg
answer: Public logo packs include PNG and JPG only. SVG, EPS, and AI are brand-owner restricted. If you need a specific format we have not packaged (vector outlines, white-only PDF, etc.), request through the Help page.

keywords: favicon, profile picture, small
answer: For favicons and social profile pictures, use the Mark alone (just the symbol, no wordmark). Render at 800px+ and export down to platform sizes. Approved colors: HCFM Blue or Yellow Gold on white, or White on HCFM Blue.

keywords: symbol meaning, what does symbol, meaning of mark, mark layers
answer:
The HCFM symbol carries five layers of meaning:
1. 10 Beads of the Rosary — totality and unity.
2. A Family of Prayer — each shape is a person in prayer.
3. Floral Character — Rose, Lily, Iris (symbolic of Mary).
4. Moon and Light — center white space emits light.
5. Welcome and Invitation — radiates outward.

keywords: 10 beads, ten beads, rosary beads, why ten
answer: The mark is composed of 10 interconnected shapes — a reference to the 10 beads of a Rosary decade. The design represents totality and unity: family members of every kind brought together in prayer, and the continuous rhythm of prayer lived daily, not occasionally.

keywords: family of prayer, person, figure
answer: Each shape in the mark represents a person in prayer — hands raised or folded. The ten shapes together form a united family. No single shape stands alone; each depends on the others. Family prayer strengthens relationships and faith.

keywords: floral, rose, lily, iris, mary connection
answer: The mark expresses a floral character — Rose, Lily, Iris, all flowers symbolic of the Virgin Mary. The petal-like shape of each unit is intentional. When you let your eye soften, the mark reads less like ten beads and more like a flower in full bloom.

keywords: moon, light center, white space mark
answer: The white space at the heart of the mark creates an optical effect that seems to emit light — a reference to the moon. In Catholic iconography, Mary is often shown standing on the moon, clothed in light. The mark carries that imagery without ever drawing it literally.

keywords: welcome, invitation, mission, outward
answer: The symbol radiates outward by design. HCFM is not inward-facing — we are mission-driven, actively reaching out to families across cultures and communities. The careful delineation of form also suggests the sense of mystery associated with the work of the Holy Spirit.

keywords: font, typography, fonts list
answer: HCFM uses three fonts. Whitney for headlines (sans-serif, 6 weights). Calluna for body text (serif, 4 weights). Playlist Script for decorative single words (Yellow Gold, White, or Marian Blue only).

keywords: body text, body font, paragraph font
answer: Use Calluna for body text. A serif font that reflects tradition, depth, and reflection. Calluna is for paragraphs, prayer text, reflections, and formal documents. Available weights: Regular, Italic, Semibold, Bold.

keywords: headline, header, display font
answer: Use Whitney for headlines, subheadings, and short text. Six weights are available: Light, Book, Medium, Semibold, Bold, Black. Whitney is clean, modern, and highly readable.

keywords: playlist script, script font, decorative font
answer: Playlist Script rules: Single words or short phrases only (3–4 words max). One element per design. Yellow Gold, White, or Marian Blue only. Never at small sizes (under 18px screen / 14pt print). Never for body text.

keywords: whitney, sans serif
answer: Whitney is HCFM's display sans-serif. Designed by Hoefler & Frere-Jones. Six weights: Light, Book, Medium, Semibold, Bold, Black. Use for headlines, navigation, buttons, callouts, captions, labels — anywhere clarity at a glance matters.

keywords: calluna, serif font
answer: Calluna is HCFM's body serif. Designed by Jos Buivenga. Four weights: Regular, Italic, Semibold, Bold. Use for paragraphs, prayer text, reflections, letterhead body, donor letters, and anywhere a serif's warmth is appropriate.

keywords: times roman, times new roman
answer: Avoid Times Roman / Times New Roman. The older SharePoint guidance suggesting Times Roman for letter body is superseded — use Calluna instead. Calluna carries the warmth Times Roman lacks.

keywords: font download, where font, get fonts
answer: Font files are on the Downloads tab under the Fonts pane. Whitney pack (368 KB), Calluna pack (252 KB), Playlist Script (52 KB), or all three in one HCFM Font Pack (660 KB).

keywords: font pairing, combination, mix fonts
answer: Default pairing: Whitney + Calluna (headline + body). For hero moments, campaign covers, donor letters, and feast-day communications, add Playlist Script for one decorative word: Whitney + Playlist Script + Calluna.

keywords: voice, tone, how to write
answer: HCFM has two registers. For the faithful: reverent, warm, family-first, narrative, named-not-abstract. For vendors: specific, direct, practical, documented. The family is the hero, prayer is the language, we are the support.

keywords: we say, preferred phrase, always say
answer: We say: "The family that prays together stays together." (Father Peyton's actual phrase.) "Holy Cross Family Ministries" spelled out on first reference, "HCFM" after. "Venerable Patrick Peyton, C.S.C." with title and order on first reference. "Father Peyton" on second reference. Country names by name.

keywords: we avoid, never say, do not say
answer: We avoid: theological jargon where plain English works. "Intercessory familial communion" when we mean family prayer. "Stakeholders" or "audience segments" for the people we serve. Hyperbole — we do not "transform lives." Generic mission language. Acronyms before they are spelled out.

keywords: donor letter, how donor letter
answer: Donor letters: warm, grateful, named. Open with "Dear friends in Christ," or "Dear [Name]," and reference something specific the donor has supported. Calluna for body. Sign with full name and title.

keywords: social caption, caption tone
answer: Social captions: plain, devotional, short. "Today, we honor the Holy Name of Jesus…" works better than "Join us as we celebrate…" Aim for 1–2 sentences plus a closing prayer or question.

keywords: imagery, photography, photo categories
answer: Five sample categories of photography: Father Patrick Peyton, ministry-center culture, Marian imagery, family prayer, Rosary imagery. Use high-quality images only. Apply dark overlays when text goes over a photo. Choose real over staged.

keywords: rule of thirds
answer: Rule of thirds: divide the image into nine equal parts with two vertical and two horizontal lines. Place the main subject at the intersections or along the lines to create a balanced scene.

keywords: leading lines
answer: Leading lines: use natural elements (rivers, roads, pathways) or man-made elements (fences, walls, staircases) to create a visual path that guides the viewer's eye to the subject.

keywords: frame within frame
answer: Frame within a frame: use natural elements like trees or rocks, or man-made objects like windows and doorways, to draw attention to the subject. Adds depth and visual interest.

keywords: filling the frame
answer: Filling the frame: move closer to the subject. The focus falls on facial expression and details that would otherwise be missed. Bonus: distracting backgrounds disappear.

keywords: shot list, event photography
answer: For HCFM events: shoot details (signs, programs, branded items), venue (before and during), attendees (candid emotional shots — both close-up and wide), speakers and special guests. A shot list separates a recap from a memory.

keywords: stock photography, staged photo
answer: Avoid stock photography. Choose real over staged: real families, real prayer, real local context. The image must reflect HCFM's actual mission, not a generic "international" aesthetic.

keywords: design element, border, fade, curve
answer: Four approved design elements: Thin Border (3-4 px max), Color Fade Effect (brand colors only), Curved Shapes, Dark Overlays. Use only one or two per design.

keywords: thin border
answer: Thin Border: a 3–4 pixel frame in a brand color. Use Muted Gold for traditional, reverent contexts. Use Yellow Gold for digital and energetic applications. Where it works: certificate edges, donor cards, prayer-card edges, formal invitations.

keywords: color fade, gradient
answer: Color Fade Effect: a gradient using only brand-palette colors. Yellow Gold to Black is the digital default. HCFM Blue to White works for formal moments. Never introduce outside colors into a brand fade.

keywords: dark overlay, photo overlay opacity
answer: Dark Overlays: black transparent layer on photos to make text readable. Adjust opacity by eye — light images with sparse text need 40 to 50 percent, busy images with longer copy need 60 to 70 percent. The rule is readability, not a number.

keywords: 60-30-10, sixty thirty ten, color ratio
answer: The 60-30-10 rule: 60% dominant color (background), 30% secondary (supporting), 10% accent (highlights). Sometimes 70-20-10 or 80-10-10 is better. The point is intentional hierarchy: one color leading, the others playing their part.

keywords: stationery, letterhead, business card, envelope
answer: Print specs: Letterhead 8.5×11 in on Strathmore 24# Bright White Wove with Pantone 871U + 2728U inks. Business card 3.5×2 in on Strathmore 130# DTC Cover. #10 envelope 9.5×4.125 in on the same letterhead stock. Mailing label 5.625×4 in on Crack-n-Peel.

keywords: letterhead template, word template
answer: Editable Word letterhead templates exist for the parent ministry and six sub-ministries (Catholic Mom, Family Rosary, Family Theater Productions, Father Peyton Prayer Guild, Museum of Family Prayer, plus the parent HCFM letterhead). Available on the Downloads tab.

keywords: easton address, mailing address
answer: HCFM Easton headquarters: 518 Washington Street, North Easton, MA 02356, USA.

answer: Instagram: static portrait 1080×1350 px · Reel/Story 1080×1920 px. Use exactly these dimensions — designs that fall short get cropped, designs that overshoot get downsized awkwardly.
